On his way home Rechungpa again met Bharima in Nepal. As to the story of Bharima correcting the jealous translator-scholars, and Rechungpa's other adventures in Nepal and India, the reader may refer to Rechungpa's Biography, in which all is told in full.
In his illuminating (Samadhi) Milarepa foresaw Rechungpa's return. He then went to Balkhu to welcome him. Thus the father and the son met again.
This is the story of Rechungpa and Dipupa."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Actually the heading is Rechungpa's Third Journey to India because there's not much about Rechungpa and Dipupa.
Any point that arises there that need's any discussion or comments?

turns.speaker: Clive
paragraphs: It doesn't say anything about him learning the art of debate.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Logic and science. It doesn't does it. Perhaps he'd forgotten all about that or perhaps as Milarepa didn't agree to him going to India to learn that perhaps it just isn't mentioned.
: He did learn some black magic and many deadly spells though.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. Perhaps he decided that would be a better way of dealing with the magicians. The attitude seems to be much the same.

turns.speaker: Adrian
paragraphs: He learns black magic and spells but he only obtains the teachings that he wants. It suggests that he just...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Obtained, yes obtained the books, has copies of the texts.

turns.speaker: Mangala
paragraphs: Another interesting point is the way prior to this you get the impression Rechungpa's gone off on his own then you realize he's got fifteen other monks.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes right. I remember when I was in Kalimpong I was taught some spells by a pagan Indian. I just remembered it. He taught me love magic, that is to say - I don't know why he appeared and he became very friendly with me. He was a Bihari and he taught me all sorts of mantras and methods of charming women. I don't know why he thought I might be interested or needed these but he taught me and so I knew how to do it. I've never actually employed these spells, and I think I've forgotten most of them now. One began [148] Om ... (Bhante chants part of the spell)... I've forgotten it!

turns.speaker: Mangala
paragraphs: Just as well! (Laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And also how to do it and under what circumstances etc., etc. So there are still these sort of people around. He was a low caste Bihari and they are the repositories of quite a lot of this black or grey magic but he turned up, I remember when I was staying at the hermitage in Kalimpong about three years after my arrival there and I forget how we became friendly but we did and he taught me all these spells and things. I had them written down at one time on I think Ola leaves but I lost them or something. I couldn't have thought very much of them.

turns.speaker: Mangala
paragraphs: Did you think they might be useful to you at some time?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I can't remember actually. No, I don't think I did, no, no. But I was always willing to learn anything! (Laughter) I had that sort of attitude. So maybe Rechungpa did this sort of thing on an even grander scale.

You notice that he meets a Yogini, a female yogi and received from her the teaching of the Buddha of Long Life. So that shows quite clearly that at that time women did function as gurus, as Tantric gurus. This yogini Magi is in fact quite well known because it was she who, not exactly was responsible for starting but who sort of systematized the churd teaching, the cutting off teaching, the teaching of cutting off the ego. She is very prominent in that connection. So one does get the occasional gifted woman Buddhist or even gifted woman teacher.
: You might even attract a few or make a few.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: (I) might even have one or two already who knows.
Any further point? Anything that we've done in this chapter because we've finished with it now. Tomorrow we'll get on to another chapter.

turns.speaker: Adrian
paragraphs: Is there any significance in the fact that the King of Ko Kom has issued Rechungpa with an invitation before and he's obviously just refused it and now he's gone to India to get something.

turns.speaker: Clive
paragraphs: I think it was that he'd given Milarepa the invitation and Milarepa had refused it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So he's happy to be able to entertain the disciple even if he hasn't been able to entertain the guru.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: It seems things aren't very much different with regard to visas and travel permits then as they are now!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Indeed! Rechungpa was asked if his travel documents were in order.
Anyway what's one's general impression of the whole chapter, of Rechungpa especially in this chapter.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: He's a bit stuck up.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: A bit stuck up.
[149] Atula: I feel I know him very well.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We're gradually doing all these chapters in which Rechungpa is mentioned. We've done some in our monthly community study here at Padmaloka and I think we did one on some occasion once before and we're doing most of the rest in the course of these two, that is to say four study seminars this month. So it will be that we have quite a lot of material on Milarepa and Rechungpa and if it's all transcribed and edited it should make a very useful volume. Because it illustrates the guru/disciple relationship in a very interesting and humorous kind of way and Rechungpa is certainly no model disciple and that might make him all the more appealing to people.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: It's quite easy to identify with Rechungpa.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I remember some time ago when I studied some of the songs of Milarepa with the women on a women's study retreat, we did those particular chapters which dealt with Milarepa's relations with different women disciples. Some of the women identified quite strongly with one particular woman disciple and others identified quite strongly with another woman disciple and this was quite remarkable, quite noticeable.
So no doubt quite a few people, not only men, maybe women too, will be able to identify with Rechungpa.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: One suspects that the author actually, whoever he was, wondering whether it was actually, whether these songs actually were Milarepa's own or whether they were a later biographers, were written with a very clear awareness of sort of archetypal significance of these characters.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But it seems very real at the same time doesn't it as though it could have well have all happened substantially as reported. Maybe they didn't exactly sing those songs in those very words but they certainly probably took up those sort of attitudes and expressed those sort of ideas.

turns.speaker: Aloka
paragraphs: Something I was wondering about if it's not just done from the point of view of making a character accessible to you from a teaching point of view is Rechungpa was one of Milarepa's main disciples.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Oh yes one of the two.

turns.speaker: Aloka
paragraphs: But he seems to have had an incredible amount of trouble with him because in other songs you get people turning up, Milarepa says a few things to them and then there's a few lines at the end saying they went off and meditated for thirty years, got enlightened and...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well perhaps one isn't told their story in full. But along with Gampopa, the author of the Jewel Ornament, Rechungpa is in fact one of the two chief disciples. You'll be interested to learn perhaps that the - I forget if it was the tenth or the twelfth - Rechung Rimpoche was a good friend of mine in Kalimpong and used to come to me for English lessons and I came to know him very well.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: Is that incarnate?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. But I don't know. He wasn't really like this Rechungpa. We certainly didn't press [profess?] to be of any high degree of spiritual [150] attainment. In fact he came to a not exactly unfortunate end, but he came to England and he got involved with a young lady, not in England in Gangtok, that's right, the daughter of a friend of mine - and in the end he got married and he came to me to confess all this and was very upset about it. That was that.

turns.speaker: Aloka
paragraphs: That doesn't sound too dissimilar to Rechungpa! (Laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ah except that in the book Rechungpa does gain enlightenment.

turns.speaker: Atula
paragraphs: Perhaps that's why Rechungpa is so important, although he is sort of up to his tricks...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: He did get there in the end, so hope and encouragement therefore is offered to all. That shouldn't be taken as an encouragement to backslide.

turns.speaker: Atula
paragraphs: No. Sort of that wherever you are you have got some possibility

turns.speaker: Aloka
paragraphs: Something that interests me to do with the same things is the fact that he's one of Milarepa's two chief disciples and that link doesn't seem to be anything to do with someone's sort of ease of spiritual progress or anything like that or how good they are at it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The good disciple is not necessarily the best disciple or the closest disciple.

turns.speaker: Aloka
paragraphs: He seems to go through the most incredible...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Nonetheless there seems to be a very definite link between him and Milarepa even though he's at times so rebellious.
Anyway let's leave it there and tomorrow we'll have a new chapter.
End of seminar
Spellchecked and put into house style Shantavira January 1999
